audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen to this telephone conversation between Janice Biggins, who works for a house renting agency, and Jon Anderson.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Flagstone.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Is that Flagstone properties?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Flagstone here.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Hello, I'm ringing just to make inquiries about renting a house.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: The name of the agent is Flagstone, so Flagstone has been written in the notes.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the first part of the conversation, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Flagstone.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Is that Flagstone properties?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Flagstone here.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Hello, I'm ringing just to make inquiries about renting a house.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: My name's Jon Anderson.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Yes, Mr Anderson.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: What sort of thing were you looking for?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Um. 2 bedroom house with garden.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Well, yes, sir.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: That shouldn't be any problem.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Just to let you know that our main areas, uh, the main areas we deal with are the city center itself.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: City center, uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: And the north suburbs.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh, well, uh we were most interested in the northern areas, actually.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: What sort of price were you thinking of?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Well, uh, could you give me some idea?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Certainly.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: It really ranges from 250 pounds per month.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Only 250 pounds?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: To about 500 pounds, depending on a number of different factors.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: What does it depend on?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Well, obviously the quality of the area, and then whether there's a garden.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Well, as I said, we'd want a garden.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: And a garage pushes up the price.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Right...
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Well, we wouldn't necessarily need one.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: I think about 350 pounds a month would be our limit.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: OK, well, would you like to have a look at a couple of properties, sir?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Yes, that would be great.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Um. Looking at our files, I think we've got 2 which might suit you.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh, hang on, I'll just get a pen.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Uh. Right.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: OK, well there's one on West Park Road, which is £325 a month.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Uh huh. Are the bills included?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Well, that one just includes the water bill.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: OK, right.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: And the second house is in Tithe road.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: I'll just spell that for you, OK?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Yep.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: TITHE Road.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Uh. Yeah, got that.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: And how much is that one?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: That's 380 pounds.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Uh. Is that including water?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: No, I'm afraid not, but it does include the telephone rental.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh, well, ha, that's not too bad then.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Uh. So.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, when would you be available to see them?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Well, uh, I'll be in town next week, say Thursday.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: No, I'm sorry.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: We don't have any availability for Thursday.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: How about Wednesday afternoon?
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Uh. OK, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Would 5 be OK?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Yes, fine.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: 5:00 it is.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Just come to the Flagstone offices.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh, before I forget.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: What sort of things do I need to get done to rent with you?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Well, the most important thing is a letter from your bank.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: And then a reference letter from your employer.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: That's OK.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: And then we would need you to give two weeks' notice of moving in.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Right, uh, 2 weeks notice.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: And what about a deposit?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: That's one month's rent, whatever the amount is.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: OK, 1 month, is that it?
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: No, sorry, one more, you will have to pay for the contract.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I'd forgotten about that.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: OK, fine.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: So I'll start arranging those, and I'll see you next week.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Yes, thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: JON

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: JANICE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Mrs Sutton's Request for Help with the Healthcare System

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You are going to hear a conversation between two women about the health system in England.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the first part of the conversation, and answer questions 11 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Mrs Sutton.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Come in.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: How are you settling in next door?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Have all your things from Canada arrived yet?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: I thought I saw a removals van outside your house yesterday afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Yes, they came yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: We spent all day yesterday arranging them.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: It's beginning to feel a bit more like home now.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Oh, that's good.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Look, come in and sit down.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Are you all right?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: You look a bit worried.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Well, I am a bit.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: I'm sorry to bother you so early, Mrs Smith, but I wonder if you could help me.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Could you tell me how I can get hold of a doctor?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Our daughter, Anna, isn't very well this morning, and I may have to call somebody out.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: She keeps being sick, and I am beginning to get a bit worried.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: I just don't know how the health system works here in England.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: All I know is that it's very different from ours back in Canada.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Well, uh, I don't know really where to start.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Let me think.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Well, the first thing you have to do is find a family doctor.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Sometimes we call them general practitioners as well.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: And register with him or her.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: If you live here, you've got to be on a doctor's list.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: If you're not, things can be a bit difficult.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Nobody will come out to you if you're not registered.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Anyway, they work in things called practices.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Sort of small groups of family doctors all working together in the same building.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Now what you've got to do this morning is register with one of them.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: There are two practices near here, so we're quite well off for doctors in this part of Manchester.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: There's the Dean End Health Center, about 10 minutes' walk away.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: And there's another practice in South Hay.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: That's about 5 minutes away, going towards the town center.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: We are registered at the Dean End one.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: But they're both OK.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: There are about six doctors in our practice and four in the other, so ours is quite big in comparison.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: And the building and everything's a bit more modern.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: South Hay is a bit old-fashioned, but the doctors are OK.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Their only problem is that they don't have a proper appointment system.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Sometimes you have to wait for ages there to see someone.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Anyway, you go to the receptionist in whichever health center, and ask her to register you with a doctor there.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: You have to fill in a form, but it doesn't take long.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Ours is called Dr Jones, and we've been going to him for years, ever since we moved here 15 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: I wouldn't say he's brilliant, but I suppose he's all right really.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: We're used to him now.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: They say he's very good with elderly people, but he does tend to get a bit impatient with children.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Listen, the one who's supposed to be really good with small children is Dr Shaw.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: I've heard lots of people say that.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: She's young and she's got small children of her own.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: So, you could try registering with her.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: And if her list is full, I heard somebody say the other day that there's a really nice young doctor at South Hay, a Dr Williams.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: He holds special clinics for people with back trouble, but that's not really your problem, is it?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 18 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the conversation, and answer questions 18 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: If you want a doctor to visit you at home, you have to ask for a home visit.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: You're supposed to do that before 10:30 in the morning, but obviously, if it's an emergency, you can phone at any time, night or day.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: It might not be your doctor that comes though.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: It's quite often one of the other doctors in the practice.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: It doesn't really seem to make much difference.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Otherwise you make an appointment to see your doctor at the health center.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: You usually get seen the same day, not always of course, but usually as I say.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: They hold surgeries between 9 and 11:30 every weekday.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: And from 4 to 6:30 Monday to Thursday.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Saturdays are only for emergencies.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: When the doctor sees you, he gives you a prescription.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: He writes what medication you need on it, and you take it to a chemist's shop.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: There's one opposite the center.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: If it's for a child under 16, you don't have to pay.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: So if it's for Anna, there's no problem.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: The same thing goes if you're unemployed or retired, or if you're pregnant, just as well, because it's not cheap.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: You pay the same price for each item the doctor has prescribed.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: At the moment, it's something like £5 per item, so you pay for the medication, but the consultation with the doctor doesn't cost you anything.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: It's completely free, as long as you're a resident here.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: You're going to be here for 3 years, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: So, there shouldn't be any question of you paying anything to see the doctor.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: So that's one less problem to worry about.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Look, Mrs Sutton, if you want, I'll sit with your daughter for half an hour, if you want to go down to the health center to register.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: It's no trouble really, don't worry.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Are you sure you wouldn't mind?
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: That would really help me a lot.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: I'll ask them if they can send someone round later to see Anna.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: I think I'll try the Dean End Center.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Don't worry about Anna.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SMITH

audioscript.text: Right, I'll be back as soon as I can.
audioscript.speaker: MRS SUTTON

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You are going to hear a conversation between two women about the health system in England.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 17.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen to the first part of the conversation, and answer questions 11 to 17.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Hello, Mrs Sutton.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Come in.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: How are you settling in next door?
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Have all your things from Canada arrived yet?
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: I thought I saw a removals van outside your house yesterday afternoon.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Yes, they came yesterday.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: We spent all day yesterday arranging them.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: It's beginning to feel a bit more like home now.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Oh, that's good.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Look, come in and sit down.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Are you all right?
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: You look a bit worried.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Well, I am a bit.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: I'm sorry to bother you so early, Mrs Smith, but I wonder if you could help me.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Could you tell me how I can get hold of a doctor?
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Our daughter, Anna, isn't very well this morning, and I may have to call somebody out.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: She keeps being sick, and I am beginning to get a bit worried.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: I just don't know how the health system works here in England.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: All I know is that it's very different from ours back in Canada.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Well, uh, I don't know really where to start.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Let me think.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Well, the first thing you have to do is find a family doctor.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Uh huh.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Sometimes we call them general practitioners as well.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Right.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: And register with him or her.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: If you live here, you've got to be on a doctor's list.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: If you're not, things can be a bit difficult.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Nobody will come out to you if you're not registered.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Anyway, they work in things called practices.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Sort of small groups of family doctors all working together in the same building.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Now what you've got to do this morning is register with one of them.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: There are two practices near here, so we're quite well off for doctors in this part of Manchester.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: There's the Dean End Health Center, about 10 minutes' walk away.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: And there's another practice in South Hay.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: That's about 5 minutes away, going towards the town center.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Uh huh.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: We are registered at the Dean End one.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: But they're both OK.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: There are about six doctors in our practice and four in the other, so ours is quite big in comparison.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: And the building and everything's a bit more modern.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: South Hay is a bit old-fashioned, but the doctors are OK.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Their only problem is that they don't have a proper appointment system.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Sometimes you have to wait for ages there to see someone.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Anyway, you go to the receptionist in whichever health center, and ask her to register you with a doctor there.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: You have to fill in a form, but it doesn't take long.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Ours is called Dr Jones, and we've been going to him for years, ever since we moved here 15 years ago.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: I wouldn't say he's brilliant, but I suppose he's all right really.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: We're used to him now.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: They say he's very good with elderly people, but he does tend to get a bit impatient with children.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Listen, the one who's supposed to be really good with small children is Dr Shaw.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: I've heard lots of people say that.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Uh huh.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: She's young and she's got small children of her own.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: So, you could try registering with her.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: And if her list is full, I heard somebody say the other day that there's a really nice young doctor at South Hay, a Dr Williams.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: He holds special clinics for people with back trouble, but that's not really your problem, is it?
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 18 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen to the rest of the conversation, and answer questions 18 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: If you want a doctor to visit you at home, you have to ask for a home visit.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: You're supposed to do that before 10:30 in the morning, but obviously, if it's an emergency, you can phone at any time, night or day.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: It might not be your doctor that comes though.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: It's quite often one of the other doctors in the practice.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: It doesn't really seem to make much difference.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Right.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Otherwise you make an appointment to see your doctor at the health center.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: You usually get seen the same day, not always of course, but usually as I say.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: They hold surgeries between 9 and 11:30 every weekday.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: And from 4 to 6:30 Monday to Thursday.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Saturdays are only for emergencies.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: I see.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: When the doctor sees you, he gives you a prescription.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: He writes what medication you need on it, and you take it to a chemist's shop.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: There's one opposite the center.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: If it's for a child under 16, you don't have to pay.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: So if it's for Anna, there's no problem.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: The same thing goes if you're unemployed or retired, or if you're pregnant, just as well, because it's not cheap.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: You pay the same price for each item the doctor has prescribed.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: At the moment, it's something like £5 per item, so you pay for the medication, but the consultation with the doctor doesn't cost you anything.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: It's completely free, as long as you're a resident here.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Uh huh.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: You're going to be here for 3 years, aren't you?
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Uh huh.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: So, there shouldn't be any question of you paying anything to see the doctor.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: So that's one less problem to worry about.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Look, Mrs Sutton, if you want, I'll sit with your daughter for half an hour, if you want to go down to the health center to register.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: It's no trouble really, don't worry.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Are you sure you wouldn't mind?
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: That would really help me a lot.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: I'll ask them if they can send someone round later to see Anna.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: I think I'll try the Dean End Center.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: Good idea.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Don't worry about Anna.
utterances.speaker: MRS SMITH

utterances.text: Right, I'll be back as soon as I can.
utterances.speaker: MRS SUTTON

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Who is Mrs Sutton worried about?
What is the name for a group of family doctors working in the same building together?

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS OR A NUMBER

items: Dr Jones is good with <input> patients.
Dr Shaw is good with small children.
Building less modern than Dean End

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS OR A NUMBER

items: Doctors start seeing patients at the Health Centre from <input> o'clock.

body.title: Which TWO groups of patients receive free medication?

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS OR A NUMBER

items: The charge for one item of medication is about £<input>

answer_key.correct_answer: daughter | her daughter | her daughter Anna | Anna | Ana

test_text.title: Jonathan Briggs' Teaching Experience and Motivations

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You're going to hear a university tutor interviewing a candidate for a place on a postgraduate diploma course in teaching geography.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first part of the interview, and answer questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Jonathan Briggs, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Do come in and sit down.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Right, well, Jonathan, as we explained in your letter, in this part of the interview, we like to talk through your application form, your experience to date, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And then in the second part, you go for a group interview.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Group interview, yes, I understand.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: So your first degree was in Economics.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, but I also did Politics as a major strand.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: And you graduated in 1989 and I see you've been doing some teaching.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I worked as a volunteer teacher in West Africa.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: I was there for almost three years in total from 1990 to 1992.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: How interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What organization was that with?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It's not one of the major ones, it's called Teach South.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I have heard of it.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It operates in several African countries, doesn't it?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And what kind of school was it?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: A rural cooperative.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Oh, a rural cooperative, how interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And what did you teach?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: A variety of things in different years.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Um. I did with forms 1 to 3, mainly geography.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: And some English with form 5, then in my final year, I took on some agricultural science with the top year, that's form 6.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Quite a variety then.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I also ran the school farm.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: How interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Before the interview continues, you have some time to look at questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the interview, and answer questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And how did you find the whole experience?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I'll be honest with you.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: At the end of the first year, I really wanted to leave and come home.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Why was that?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, I was very homesick at first, and missed my family.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, I can understand that.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And I also found it frustrating to have so few teaching resources.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: But I did decide to stay, and in the end, I extended my tour to a third year.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Things must have looked up then.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, we set up a very successful project breeding cattle to sell locally.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And then after a lot of hard work, we finally got funds for new farm buildings.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: And you wanted to see things through.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: And that's why you want to train to teach geography.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I've had a couple of jobs since then, but I now realize I like teaching best.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: And I chose geography because it's my favorite subject.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: And also because I think it has so many useful applications.
audioscript.speaker: JB

audioscript.text: Well, you certainly have had some interesting work experience.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I'll ask you now to go on to the next stage of the interview.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Back Pain: Causes, Aggravations and Remedies

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You are going to hear a radio talk on back pain, given by Doctor Paula Clayberg of Liverpool's Wilton clinic.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the talk, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today's Health Counsel is presented by Paula Clayburg, who is the chief counselor at Liverpool's famous pain clinic, the Wilton Clinic.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Uh. Paula.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Do you know what Prince Charles, Seve Ballesteros, and Elizabeth Taylor have in common?
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: They all suffer from chronic back pain.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: In fact, bad backs are one of the most common health problems today, affecting people in all walks of life.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: The most recent available figures show that about a quarter of a million people are incapacitated with back pain every day.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: And many sufferers don't know the cause or the solution to their problem.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: The majority of our patients at the clinic tend to be women.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: They are especially vulnerable because of pregnancy, but also because of osteoporosis.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Which I personally believe to be the major cause of problems for women.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: I have many women patients who say they have completely given up exercise, because the pain makes them so miserable.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: But of course, that starts up a vicious circle.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Bed rest, giving up exercise, and pain killers are traditional responses to back pain but, although there are many excellent drugs on the market, at our clinic, we're beginning to realize the unique benefits of relaxation therapy.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Other specialists in the field make a strong case for certain types of exercise, but in our experience, they are easily mishandled, and can lead to more harm than good.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Now let's look at some of the reasons why back pain is developing into such a unique menace.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: In general, the body is pretty good at self repair.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: A strain or a blow to a limb, though painful at the time, generally resolves itself.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: But the body's response to back injury can be very counterproductive.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: When pain strikes, we attempt to keep the back as immobile as possible, which makes the muscles tense up.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Research shows that they often go into spasm,
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: which causes further twisting of the spine; a vicious circle is underway.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: The second mistake we often make when stricken with extreme back pain is to go to bed and stay there.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Although at the clinic, we recognize that a short rest in bed can be helpful.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Up to 2 days, any longer makes our back muscles become weaker and unable to hold up our spine.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: The pain therefore becomes worse.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Another problem is being overweight.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Anyone a stone or more overweight, who already has back pain, is not doing himself any favors.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Though it won't actually set it off in the first place, the weight will increase the strain and make things worse.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: The British diet could be partially to blame for the increase in back pain.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Over the last 10 years, the average weight of men has risen by 11 pounds.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: And of women by 9 pounds.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: So much for the causes and aggravations of pain, but what can we do to help?
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: There are many ways in which simple day to day care can make all the difference.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: The first point to watch of course is weight.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: If you are overweight, a diet will make all the difference.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Also, studies have shown that just 1 hour sitting in a slouched position can strain ligaments in the back which can take months to heal.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: At the clinic, we have come to the conclusion that the major cause of the problem is not with the design of chairs, as some have suggested, but in the way we sit in them.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: It can be useful to get special orthopedic chairs, but remember the most important improvement should be in our posture.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Another enemy of your back is of course your beds.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: If your bed doesn't give enough support, back muscles and ligaments work all night trying to correct spinal alignment.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: So you wake up with a tired aching back.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Try out an orthopedic mattress or a spring slatted bed.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Research shows that both can be beneficial for certain types of back pain.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Another hazard for your back are the shock waves which travel up your spine when you walk, known as heel strike.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: A real find for our patients has been the shock absorbing shoe insert, a cheap but very effective solution.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: And you might be better off avoiding shoes with heels higher than one and a half inches.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Though absolutely flat shoes can be a solution for some, others find their posture suffers.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Finally, a word about the state-of-the-art relief, the TENS machine, a small battery-powered gadget, which delivers subliminal electrical pulses to the skin.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: Our experience indicates that your money is better spent on the more old-fashioned remedies.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA CLAYBURG

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You are going to hear a radio talk on back pain, given by Doctor Paula Clayberg of Liverpool's Wilton clinic.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully to the talk, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: According to the speaker, the main cause of back pain in women is
As treatment for back pain the Clinic mainly recommends
The back is different from other parts of the body because
options: it is usually better at self-repair.
a back injury is usually more painful.
its response to injury often results in more damage.

items.title: Bed rest is advised
options: for a maximum of two days.
for pain lasting more than two days.

items.title: Being overweight
options: is a major source of back pain.
